facts_disclosed: Planning to start a side gig installing EV home chargers

turns.content: yo so I been thinkin about somethin and I wanna run it by you real quick

ok so you know how everyones gettin electric cars now right like teslas and rivians and whatever. well all those people need chargers at their house and most electricians around here arent even doin that work yet
Yeah, that's a growing market for sure. Home EV charger installations are picking up fast, especially in areas where EV adoption is climbing. Are you thinking about getting into that space?

dude thats exactly what im thinkin. like I already know the electrical side of it right I been doin panel work and runnin circuits for years now with my dads crew so its not like I gotta learn anything new its literally just a 240 volt circuit and a mounted box

That makes sense. If you already have the electrical fundamentals down from your day job, the actual charger installation is pretty straightforward from a technical standpoint. The real questions would be around the business side — licensing, permits, insurance, finding customers. Have you looked into any of that?

yeah kinda so heres the thing my boy marcus not the one from the rink a different marcus he started doin this like 6 months ago on weekends and hes makin like 500 bucks a pop. five hundred dollars for like 3 hours of work bro thats insane money

That's a solid rate per job, especially for weekend work. $500 for a few hours is well above typical side gig pay. Has your friend shared anything about how he's getting his customers — word of mouth, online ads, something else?

he said mostly nextdoor and like facebook marketplace posts and then after the first few jobs people just started referrin him to their neighbors and stuff. he said the demand is wicked crazy like he has to turn people away sometimes cause hes booked up

That's a really good sign if he's already turning away work after only six months. Organic referrals are the best kind of marketing, and they cost nothing. The fact that demand is outstripping his capacity means there's definitely room for another installer in the area.

right thats what I was thinkin like theres enough work for both of us easy. and honestly I could probably charge a little less than the big companies do and still make bank cause my overhead is basically nothin its just me and my tools

That's a real competitive advantage. Big electrical contractors have overhead — office, employees, trucks, marketing budgets — so they price accordingly. You could undercut them and still make great margins. One thing worth thinking about: do you need any specific certifications or permits in Massachusetts to do this independently, or would you be working under an existing license?

yeah thats the one thing I gotta figure out. like I passed my journeyman exam so thats huge but I think to pull permits on my own I might need to look into it more. but im thinkin worst case I could do it through my dads company on the side you know keep it legit

Running it through your dad's company could actually be the smart play, at least to start. You'd be covered under their existing licenses and insurance, and you could still pocket the labor. It also means you wouldn't have to deal with setting up your own business entity right away. Have you talked to your dad about it yet?

nah not yet I wanna have like a real plan before I bring it to him cause you know dads they wanna hear numbers not just vibes lol but im wicked excited about it bro this could be a real thing for me. like actual good money on weekends instead of just sittin around playin warzone

That's a smart approach — coming to him with a plan shows you're serious about it. You could put together some rough numbers: average job price, estimated time per install, cost of materials, how many you could realistically do per weekend. If the math works out as well as your friend's experience suggests, it's going to be a pretty compelling pitch. Good luck with it.
